text: An emergency fund of 3-6 months of essential expenses is the foundation of financial security - it turns a crisis into an inconvenience.
The Rule of 72: divide 72 by an annual interest rate to estimate how many years money takes to double - at 8% growth, savings double roughly every 9 years.
Compound interest works against you on credit cards: paying only the minimum on a high-interest balance can stretch a small debt into years of payments.
'Pay yourself first': automating a transfer to savings on payday makes saving the default instead of whatever happens to be left over.
The 50/30/20 guideline splits take-home pay into 50% needs, 30% wants, and 20% savings and debt payoff - a simple starting budget that needs no spreadsheet.
Keeping credit card balances below about 30% of the card's limit (credit utilization) is one of the biggest levers on a credit score.
Diversification - spreading money across many investments, e.g. through broad index funds - reduces risk without necessarily reducing returns.
Cash quietly loses value to inflation: at 5% inflation, money kept under the mattress loses about half its purchasing power in 14 years.
Insurance is for catastrophes you couldn't afford to cover yourself; insuring small, affordable losses usually costs more than it returns.
When sending money abroad, compare the TOTAL cost: a 'zero-fee' transfer often hides its charge in a worse exchange rate. The margin on the rate matters more than the fee.
Beware lifestyle inflation: if spending rises to match every raise, income grows but savings never do. Banking part of each raise keeps progress automatic.
Any investment promising high returns with little or no risk is a red flag - 'guaranteed' outsized returns are the signature of Ponzi schemes and scams.
Small recurring charges add up: auditing subscriptions once a year often frees up more money than weeks of penny-pinching.
Paying off a 20%-interest debt is a guaranteed 20% return - usually the best 'investment' available to anyone carrying card debt.
Time in the market beats timing the market: missing just the handful of best days in a decade can erase a large share of an investor's total returns.
People who write down specific savings goals ('RD$X by December for Y') save more than those with vague intentions - specificity turns a wish into a plan.
